title: Adenovirus: pharyngoconjunctival fever outbreak
vignette: During the course of a week at an overnight summer camp, 4 children age 7-9 are sent to the camp health center. They each have fever, cough, congestion, sore throat, and red eyes. Physical examination of the children shows bilateral conjunctival injection and an erythematous oropharynx. Auscultation of one child also reveals crackles in the left lower lung field. All the children's symptoms improve over 7 days with supportive care. Which of the following is the most likely cause of the outbreak?

clues.text: 4 children age 7-9 are sent to the camp health center
clues.note: A cluster of cases in a crowded setting (summer camp) suggests an outbreak of a contagious respiratory virus. Adenovirus classically causes outbreaks in camps, barracks, and daycares.

clues.text: conjunctival injection and an erythematous oropharynx
clues.note: Fever + pharyngitis + conjunctivitis is 'pharyngoconjunctival fever,' the hallmark adenovirus syndrome. Conjunctival injection plus sore throat is the classic combination.

whatMatters: A camp outbreak of fever + pharyngitis + conjunctivitis (pharyngoconjunctival fever), self-limited, occasionally with pneumonia = adenovirus. The conjunctivitis-plus-pharyngitis combination in a crowded setting is the discriminator from the other respiratory viruses.
strategy: Match the syndrome to the virus: adenovirus = pharyngoconjunctival fever + outbreaks in crowded quarters (camps/barracks); coxsackie = hand-foot-mouth/herpangina; influenza = high fever/myalgia in winter; RSV = bronchiolitis in infants; norovirus = vomiting/diarrhea. Conjunctivitis + pharyngitis cluster = adenovirus.

eliminations.label: coxsackievirus — hand-foot-mouth/herpangina, not conjunctivitis
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: coxsackievirus causes hand-foot-and-mouth disease (oral + palm/sole vesicles) and herpangina (posterior oropharyngeal vesicles). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it doesn't cause the fever-pharyngitis-CONJUNCTIVITIS triad; no conjunctival injection. ✅ Correct if: there were vesicles on hands/feet and mouth or painful posterior oral vesicles.

eliminations.label: influenza — winter, high fever/myalgia, no conjunctivitis cluster
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: influenza causes abrupt high fever, myalgias, cough, malaise, usually in winter. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it doesn't characteristically cause pharyngoconjunctival fever, and the seasonality (summer camp) and conjunctivitis fit adenovirus better. ✅ Correct if: a winter outbreak of high fever, myalgia, and cough were described.

eliminations.label: norovirus — GI outbreak, not respiratory
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: norovirus causes outbreaks of acute vomiting and watery diarrhea (cruises, camps). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: the symptoms here are respiratory + conjunctival, not gastrointestinal. ✅ Correct if: the cluster had vomiting/diarrhea (gastroenteritis).

eliminations.label: parvovirus B19 — erythema infectiosum, not this
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: parvovirus B19 causes erythema infectiosum ('slapped cheek') in children and aplastic crises/arthropathy. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it doesn't cause pharyngoconjunctival fever or respiratory outbreaks with conjunctivitis. ✅ Correct if: there were a slapped-cheek facial rash or aplastic crisis in sickle cell.

eliminations.label: RSV — bronchiolitis in infants, not pharyngoconjunctivitis
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: RSV causes bronchiolitis and pneumonia, predominantly in infants/young children (wheezing, winter). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it doesn't produce the pharyngitis-conjunctivitis syndrome in school-age camp kids; age/season and findings differ. ✅ Correct if: an infant had wheezing bronchiolitis in winter.

reasoning: Cluster in a crowded camp = contagious respiratory virus outbreak
mechanism: Adenovirus is a nonenveloped, double-stranded DNA virus transmitted by respiratory droplets, fecal-oral route, and direct contact (including contaminated water), causing year-round disease and outbreaks in crowded settings (camps, military barracks, daycares). It replicates in respiratory and conjunctival epithelium, producing pharyngoconjunctival fever (fever, pharyngitis, conjunctivitis) and, in some, pneumonia. Most infections are self-limited.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Micro > DNA viruses: adenovirus (nonenveloped dsDNA; pharyngoconjunctival fever, conjunctivitis, outbreaks in camps/barracks, hemorrhagic cystitis). Contrast coxsackie, influenza, RSV, norovirus, parvovirus.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Map the syndrome (fever + pharyngitis + conjunctivitis in a camp cluster) to adenovirus. The conjunctivitis-plus-pharyngitis combination is the discriminator.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on pharyngoconjunctival fever, conjunctival injection, herpangina/hand-foot-mouth, bronchiolitis? Tell me which and I'll explain.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Influenza and RSV are generic 'respiratory virus' baits, and coxsackie tempts via oral lesions — but only adenovirus gives the fever-pharyngitis-CONJUNCTIVITIS camp outbreak.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: If you blanked, lock one association: adenovirus = pharyngoconjunctival fever + crowded-quarters outbreak (and swimming-pool conjunctivitis).

firstAid.detail: Micro > DNA viruses: adenovirus (pharyngoconjunctival fever, conjunctivitis, pneumonia, hemorrhagic cystitis; outbreaks in crowded settings).
firstAid.location: Micro > DNA viruses: adenovirus (pharyngoconjunctival fever, conjunctivitis, pneumonia, hemorrhagic cystitis; outbreaks in crowded settings).

firstAid.detail: Micro > coxsackievirus, influenza, RSV, norovirus, parvovirus B19 — signature syndromes for differentiation.
firstAid.location: Micro > coxsackievirus, influenza, RSV, norovirus, parvovirus B19 — signature syndromes for differentiation.

firstAid.detail: Micro > naked vs enveloped DNA viruses; adenovirus among the nonenveloped dsDNA group.
firstAid.location: Micro > naked vs enveloped DNA viruses; adenovirus among the nonenveloped dsDNA group.

memoryHook: Camp kids with sore throat + red eyes = adenovirus's pharyngoconjunctival fever (it loves crowded bunks and swimming pools).
